item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic, wish I had read this sooner.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, I would definitely buy some of these

item.title: Unnecessary random thought
item.content:encoded:
The foundation is currently incinerating the cards rather than allowing them to get 'raptured.' What are the moral ramifications of deliberately preventing someone from collecting their 'eternal reward'?

also I really wanna know what an ethics department for the foundation would look like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An oil painting of a staircase set into the clouds ascending toward an archetypical Abrahamic image of Heaven - "If tears could build a stairway / and memories a lane / I'd walk right up to Heaven / and bring you home again

I'm not that well versed in other religions, but to the best of my knowledge the Pearly Gates version of Heaven is really only a thing in Christian imagery, not in the imagery of any other Abrahamic religions.

item.title: Re: I liked it but...
item.content:encoded: Is this what it passes for "trolling" nowadays?

item.title: I liked it but...
item.content:encoded: Can you make one of the cards bisexual. This article is a little "exclusive". But otherwise great article :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think we need a separate page so people can make their own. A few rules, though…
1) Must be formatted like the original addendum
2) Number of cards shown must range from 3 to 10 (for those that have a story to tell)
3) Cards must be religious in nature (the religion can, of course, be fictional)
You don't have to make one if you don't want to, but I think it would help build the SCP. Anyways, have your stinking upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Older" could be referring to a ~29 year old, since they're approaching their death age.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, so, if everyone welcomes death at Age 30, because it lets them move on to the afterlife where you… uh… get to control the living… then how does the "You know you're getting older" card work? Presumably the recipient of the card is only still alive after 30 because that's what their ancestors are forcing them to do, yes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading through the procedures and description, I couldn't tell why this was so popular. Nothing to grab me – everything before the addendum read as a thing that does a thing.

And then I read the log.
This is where you sold it. You sold it hard.

Of note are the religions you made up. The one that caught my eye wasn't the children running to the hooded white figure (although that is some fantastic imagery); it was the card with the puppet-son. It gave me goosebumps and made me sit down for a good five minutes, mulling over that depiction and wondering why it scared me so much.

I'm still trying to figure that out. It is clear, however, why I've upvoted this piece.

item.title: Re: Testing the cards with each other
item.content:encoded: A test where they place an anomalous card preaching a different religion at the other end of a row would be interesting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to admit, of all the places I expected to see the name "Kandarin" other than NationStates, this was not what I came up with first. Love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that, post office, or even wartime post office, should be one thing to be mentioned somewhere in the article

item.title: Testing the cards with each other
item.content:encoded:
Do affected cards proselytize other instances of the SCP?

Could the cards be contained by causing a religious war or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Outside: A gold outline of a slaughtered sheep with its liver removed and prominently displayed - "The Omens are Clear…"
Inside: "Your fate is great this year! Happy Birthday!"

^Upvoted. I love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because I do not understand Western religions, it is very difficult for me to understand it.QwQ

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last one gives me a very Logan's Run feel…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously, where's the collab tag?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While including Fifthist, Sarkic, or Broken God-themed greeting cards here might've been too easy, hell, I'd still want to see 'em.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Geez, what the heck is that last religion? I'll be real disappointed if it's made up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You already had my upvote when I realized these cards were all proselytizing each other so that they could be part of the Glorious Hallmark Rapture.
But then I saw the theme of the last religion in the addendum, and my +1 was much more firmly secured.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
investigate reports of offensive … greeting cards

For the first few moments after reading that I interpreted it as greeting cards which can be used as weapons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm! This is really neat - especially enjoying Vesta. Good job. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a clear idea of what the religion in the third examples believes / practices, but like most religious greeting cards, it's not so much about evangelism as an assumption that the recipient of the card is already a devout believer in the religion in question and doesn't need an explanation of what its tenets are. I think that there are still enough hints to clue out some of those tenets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So was that last one supposed to be the Church of Euthanasia?

Anyway, well done. This concept could have gone either way, and you pulled it off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Last three cards aren't so much "alternate religions and evangelistic efforts" as "being generically creepy".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Final card gave me the shivers, and I'm not quite sure why.

Loved the log. Enthusiastic upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quirky and fun, I like it. That final card is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept was somewhat interesting overall, but I really liked the final three sample cards. I found them pretty creepy. I assume they were for the entirely fabricated religions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…push the envelope a little bit further.

I love that pun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch. I've edited it to "Haredi Orthodox".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but I have one gripe with it: I'm not sure "Ultra-Orthodox" Jews are actually a thing. Small quibble, but you may want to fact check.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to everybody who critiqued this, especially thedeadlymoose and Montala for picking through the examples and urging me to push the envelope a bit further.

I took the picture in this article when I came across the scene pictured while grocery shopping one day. I still have no idea why the greeting cards were gone or why one was left behind, but this seemed like as good of an explanation as any.
